title: Sweat Stain on Silk — Mild Enzyme with Acid Rinse
stainChemistry: Sweat is a complex mixture of salts (NaCl), proteins (primarily urea and proteins from skin), and sometimes deodorant/antiperspirant chemicals. On silk, proteins bind to the protein fiber structure while salts can crystallize and cause yellowing over time. Lipase enzymes digest the protein components; salt requires plain water rinse. Aged sweat stains oxidize and become insoluble—early treatment is critical.
whyThisWorks: Lipase enzymes break down the protein and lipid components of sweat without damaging silk's delicate protein structure. Cool water rinses salt residue. Mild acid (28% Acetic Acid (diluted)) post-treatment stabilizes any remaining residues and prevents oxidation. Speed matters—fresh sweat responds to this protocol reliably.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse the sweat-stained area with cool water to dissolve and remove salt residue. This is important—salt crystallization causes yellowing over time. Use gentle spray, don't rub. Support fabric underneath.

Apply lipase enzyme solution. Lipase breaks down the protein and lipid components in sweat. Do NOT use Protein—it will damage silk protein fiber. Allow 10-15 minutes for the enzyme to work. You may see slight color change as residue is digested.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: OxiClean MaxForce Enzyme Booster or Seventh Generation Enzyme Detergent

spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with cool water to remove all enzyme solution. Be very gentle—silk weakens when wet. Residual enzyme can cause discoloration or fiber damage if left on the fabric.

spottingProtocol.agent: Mild acid rinse in 1 cup cool water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply vinegar solution. Mild acid stabilizes any remaining protein residues and prevents oxidative darkening. This is what creates the characteristic yellow underarm staining over time—the acid rinse prevents this. Blot gently.
Rinse multiple times with cool water. Remove all vinegar residue. Be very gentle—silk is weakened by water. Do not wring or squeeze.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Flat surface or very loose hanging
spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay flat or hang very loosely to dry. Never wring silk. As fabric dries, the protein structure strengthens. Monitor the treated area—stain should fade completely.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Water rinse + lipase enzyme + vinegar rinse works best within 24 hours. Very high success rate.
WITHIN DAYS: Still effective up to 1 week. After this, sweat proteins begin oxidizing and becoming darker/more insoluble.
OLDER: Professional dry cleaner recommended. Set sweat stains (weeks old) may require specialized treatment.
materialWarnings: NEVER use Protein enzymes on silk—will digest silk protein fiber structure and cause damage.
NEVER use hot water—denatures silk protein and sets sweat stains permanently.
NEVER use chlorine bleach—destroys silk fiber integrity.
NEVER use strong alkali—ammonia or lye damage silk irreversibly.
Be extremely gentle when wet—silk loses significant strength when saturated.
escalation: If lipase enzyme and vinegar treatment don't fully remove the stain, professional dry cleaner can assess. Aged sweat stains sometimes require specialized oxidizing treatments that must be applied carefully to delicate silk.

household.name: OxiClean MaxForce Enzyme Booster
household.note: Lipase enzyme specifically (not Protein). Breaks down sweat proteins and lipids without damaging silk.

household.name: Seventh Generation Free & Clear Enzyme Laundry Detergent
household.amazonSearchQuery: Seventh Generation free clear enzyme laundry detergent
household.note: Lipase-based detergent. Can be used as pre-soak for fresh sweat stains on silk.

household.name: 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) (5% acetic acid)
household.note: Critical final step. Stabilizes any remaining protein and prevents oxidative yellowing.

professional.name: Specialty Protein-Stain Removal
professional.note: Professional shops can use specialized enzyme treatments safe on silk for set sweat stains.

customerExplanation: Sweat is a mixture of salt and body proteins. The proteins bind to silk's protein structure while salt crystallizes and causes yellowing. Lipase enzymes digest the protein components—the same enzymes that break down oils in your digestive system. Then we rinse the salt away and apply a mild acid to stabilize any remaining residue and prevent oxidation.
commonMistakes: Using hot water—denatures sweat proteins and sets them permanently into silk protein fiber; causes yellowing.
Using Protein enzymes on silk—will damage the silk protein structure in addition to sweat; causes irreversible fiber weakening.
Skipping the vinegar rinse—leaves protein residues unstable; over time, they oxidize and darken, creating the classic yellow underarm staining.
Waiting more than 1 week before treating—sweat proteins oxidize through air exposure and become much harder to remove.
scienceNote: Sweat contains urea, ammonia, and proteins that form hydrogen bonds with silk's protein chains. Lipase (not Protein) preferentially cleaves the ester bonds in lipid components of sweat without attacking the amide bonds in silk protein. The mild acid rinse afterward protonates any remaining nitrogen-based residues (from urea/ammonia), making them less likely to oxidize into the yellow or brown compounds that give aged sweat stains their characteristic discoloration.
callPro: If enzyme and vinegar treatment doesn't fully resolve the stain, or if the stain has visibly oxidized to yellow or brown, call a professional. Do not attempt bleach or repeated treatments—risk of weakening silk protein increases significantly.
solventNote: Professional shops: Lipase enzyme (not Protein) is correct choice for sweat on silk. Allow 10-15 minutes dwell time. Vinegar rinse is critical to prevent oxidative darkening—this is the secret to preventing set-in yellow underarm staining. Cool water throughout. Do not use heat, bleach, or strong alkali. Silk's delicate protein structure requires careful handling.
